title: Part 1: Sliding Window — Minimum Window Substring (LC 76)

cues.label: Key insight
cues.headline: Never reset the window
cues.narration: The right pointer only moves forward, the left pointer only moves forward. No character is visited more than twice.

cues.label: Two dictionaries
cues.headline: Need and window maps
cues.narration: Need counts what t requires. Window counts what the current range contains. Formed tracks how many characters are satisfied.

cues.label: Formed count
cues.headline: Running tally
cues.narration: When window[c] reaches need[c], increment formed. When formed equals distinct needed characters, the window is valid.

cues.label: Shrink phase
cues.headline: Minimize the window
cues.narration: When valid, record the minimum, then remove the leftmost character and check if formed drops. Stop shrinking when the window is no longer valid.

cues.label: Expand again
cues.headline: Continue right
cues.narration: After shrinking invalidates the window, continue expanding right to find the next valid window.

cues.label: The template
cues.headline: Expand then shrink
cues.narration: This expand-then-shrink loop is the core of every sliding window problem. Adapt the validity check and the bookkeeping for each variant.

cues.label: Edge cases
cues.headline: Three gotchas
cues.narration: Three edge cases: t longer than s, duplicate characters in t, and tracking the start index of the best window, not just the length.

cues.label: Complexity
cues.headline: O(|s| + |t|)
cues.narration: Each character in s enters and leaves the window at most once. Building the need map is O(|t|). Space is O(alphabet size).
